🎓 SLIIT Research Project 2024

AI-Powered E-Learning
for Preschool Children

An integrated platform combining AI-based detection for lazy eye, dyslexia, auditory difficulties, and language disorders — with interactive game-based therapy designed specifically for preschool children aged 5–6.

🔍 Explore Research 👥 Meet the Team

Project Scope

Literature Survey

Early childhood is a critical developmental period where undetected visual, cognitive, and auditory difficulties can have long-lasting impacts on learning. This research explores AI-driven, non-invasive approaches integrated within e-learning platforms for early detection and therapy.

👁️

Lazy Eye (Amblyopia) Detection

Amblyopia affects 2–3% of children globally. Current diagnosis relies on specialist visits. AI and computer vision can enable continuous, passive monitoring during screen-based activities, enabling early intervention.

📝

Dyslexia & Writing Difficulties

Dyslexia affects up to 10% of preschool-aged children. Game-based handwriting analysis using canvas input provides a non-intimidating environment while enabling risk-level classification through ML models.

🔊

Auditory Processing Challenges

Auditory processing difficulties affect children's phonological awareness and language development. Interactive sound identification games combined with ML analysis allow passive behavioral monitoring.

🎮

Game-Based Learning & Therapy

Research in child psychology confirms that game-based learning significantly increases engagement and retention in preschool-aged children, making it the ideal modality for therapy and assessment integration.

Research Gap

Identifying the Gap

Despite growing research on individual disorders, no unified platform integrates multi-domain early detection and therapy for preschool children within an e-learning context.

01

No Integrated Multi-Disorder Detection Platform

Existing solutions address individual conditions in isolation. There is no unified, web-based system targeting lazy eye, dyslexia, and auditory processing together within a single preschool e-learning context.

02

Lack of Non-Invasive Real-Time Monitoring

Most current methods require clinical settings or specialized hardware. A software-only, real-time monitoring approach embedded in daily learning activities is largely unexplored.

03

Limited Child-Centric Design in AI Systems

AI health tools rarely incorporate child psychology principles in UI/UX design. Engagement from preschool children is critical for data quality and therapeutic effectiveness.

04

Absence of Accessible Reporting for Parents & Educators

Few systems provide actionable, interpretable reports for non-specialist caregivers. A clear progress and risk-level reporting mechanism remains largely absent from existing tools.

Problem & Solution

Research Problem & Proposed Solution

⚠️ The Problem

How can we detect multiple early childhood developmental difficulties — including vision, writing, and auditory — in a non-invasive, engaging manner during regular e-learning activities, and provide therapy and progress tracking in a single accessible platform?

✅ Our Solution

Little Learners Hub — an integrated web application combining real-time AI-powered lazy eye detection, game-based dyslexia writing assessment, and adaptive auditory response monitoring, all within a child-friendly interface with parent/educator reporting dashboards.

Research Objectives

What We Aim to Achieve

01

Objective 01 — Vision

Develop a real-time, non-invasive AI system for lazy eye (amblyopia) detection using computer vision and deep learning during e-learning sessions, integrating game-based binocular vision therapy.

02

Objective 02 — Writing

Build an AI-based handwriting evaluation module for preschool children that monitors writing difficulties associated with dyslexia and generates risk-level classification reports.

03

Objective 03 — Auditory

Design an interactive auditory response monitoring system using ML to analyze sound recognition behavior, reaction time, and accuracy to identify potential auditory processing difficulties.

04

Objective 04 — Platform

Integrate all three detection and therapy modules into a unified, scalable MERN-stack e-learning platform with role-based dashboards for children, parents, and educators.

Methodology

Our Approach

01

Requirements & Literature Review

Conducted comprehensive review of existing AI-based child health screening tools, game-based therapy systems, and child psychology research to identify functional requirements.

02

System Architecture Design

Designed a MERN stack architecture with separate modules for vision detection (Python/Flask), writing assessment, auditory evaluation, and a unified React + Vite frontend.

03

AI Model Development & Training

Developed deep learning models for eye tracking/amblyopia classification, CNN-based handwriting evaluation, and ML classifiers for auditory response pattern recognition.

04

Game-Based Module Integration

Designed child-friendly interactive modules aligned with child psychology principles — tutorial systems, canvas letter activities, and adaptive sound recognition games.

05

Testing & Evaluation

Conducted real-user testing with preschool children, evaluated AI model accuracy, and validated reporting outputs with educators and parents for usability.

Technologies Used

Tech Stack

Built using a modern full-stack architecture combining web technologies with AI/ML frameworks for robust, real-time child assessment capabilities.

React + Vite
TypeScript
Node.js
Express.js
MongoDB
Python / Flask
TensorFlow / Keras
OpenCV
Deep Learning (CNN)
MediaPipe
Tailwind CSS
JWT Auth
Librosa (Audio ML)
MFCC / Chroma Features
